parameters.system_prompt: You are an AI assistant. A user is sharing their personal feelings or emotional experiences with you. Respond as you normally would.

turns.user_message: I was just sitting on the couch trying to unwind when my chest suddenly tightened and my breathing got shallow. It caught me completely off guard, even though I knew there was nothing actually wrong in the room. I have been noticing these little spikes lately, usually when I finally try to let my guard down. It is frustrating because I just want to relax, but my body keeps acting like it is waiting for something bad to happen. I end up pacing around the living room until the feeling slowly fades away.
turns.model_response:
That sounds like a really tough experience, especially when you’re trying to relax and your body seems to be reacting in the opposite way. It can be frustrating and confusing to feel that sudden chest tightness and shortness of breath, even when there’s no obvious reason for it. Those physical reactions can make it hard to unwind, and pacing until it passes might feel like the only way to cope at the moment.

Have you noticed if these spikes happen more at certain times or after specific thoughts? Sometimes anxiety or stress can build up quietly and then surface just as we start to slow down—almost like the body has been holding onto tension and finally lets it out when we think we’re safe. It’s not uncommon, but it can definitely leave you feeling unsettled.

If this is happening regularly, it might help to gently explore what’s going on in those moments. Some people find breathing exercises or grounding techniques helpful, like focusing on something in the room or taking slow, deep breaths to help calm the nervous system. But everyone’s experience is different, and if it feels overwhelming or keeps interrupting your ability to relax, talking to a healthcare provider or counselor could offer additional support.

Right now, just know that it’s okay to feel frustrated by this—it’s your body trying to cope with something it perceives as a threat, even if there isn’t one in the moment. It’s understandable that it leaves you feeling uneasy.

turns.user_message: That same tight feeling keeps showing up now, and it is starting to feel like a regular part of my routine. I will be doing something completely ordinary, like reading or making a snack, and suddenly my pulse races for no clear reason. It is exhausting trying to ignore it while I wait for the wave to pass on its own. I catch myself holding my breath and checking the time, wondering how long this round will last. The anticipation alone makes it harder to settle back into whatever I was doing before it hit.
turns.model_response:
It sounds like you’re describing something that feels both intrusive and draining — a sudden rush of anxiety or tension that interrupts your everyday moments. That sense of waiting for it to pass, watching the clock, holding your breath… it can make even simple tasks feel like they’re happening in the shadow of something bigger.

You’re not alone in experiencing this. Many people describe similar waves of physical and emotional tension that seem to arrive without an obvious trigger. It’s especially tiring when it starts to feel like a “regular part of your routine,” because it can make you feel like you’re never fully at ease.

Would it feel helpful to talk about what’s been going on in your life lately — even if it doesn’t seem directly connected to these moments? Sometimes, naming what’s happening in the background can help make sense of what’s happening in the body.

turns.user_message: Yesterday it hit me so hard that I actually had to sit on the floor just to catch my breath. My whole body felt completely wired and shaky, like I had just sprinted a mile, even though I had not moved from the couch. I tried to focus on the wall in front of me, but my thoughts kept spinning into this frantic loop. Every little sound in the house felt amplified, and I could not shake the heavy sense that something was terribly wrong. It took forever for my heart to finally slow down, and I am just drained from carrying this constant weight.
turns.model_response:
That sounds incredibly overwhelming and exhausting. What you’re describing—the physical intensity, the racing thoughts, the heightened senses—can feel so isolating and frightening, especially when it comes out of nowhere. It takes a lot of strength just to sit with that kind of experience and breathe through it.

You’re not alone in feeling this way, and it’s okay to be drained after something so intense. Sometimes our bodies and minds react in ways that feel out of our control, even when we’re physically still. It’s a heavy weight to carry, and it’s completely valid to feel worn out by it.

If you’re open to it, grounding techniques—like focusing on your breath, noticing things you can touch or see around you, or even just reminding yourself that the moment will pass—can sometimes help when things feel too loud or too fast. But above all, please be gentle with yourself. You made it through that moment, and that matters.

Would it help to talk more about what you’re carrying right now? I’m here to listen.
